meta.title: FirstPassRx
meta.disclaimer: A point-of-care reference, not a substitute for the payer's current formulary or clinical judgment. Formularies change quarterly; confirm every agent and restriction against the linked source before prescribing.

guides.label: MA · Inhalers
guides.tagline: Pick a plan and inhaler class. Start with the green answer.

payers.name: Blue Cross Blue Shield of Massachusetts

classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

glossary.short: Insurer sign-off needed before the drug is covered.
glossary.definition: A requirement that the prescriber get the plan's approval before it will pay for a drug. Until it's approved, the pharmacy claim rejects — which is the round-trip this tool helps you skip.

glossary.short: The plan forces the brand; the generic rejects.
glossary.definition: Some plans prefer a specific brand and won't cover its generic equivalent. You must write the brand and mark "dispense as written" or the pharmacy claim rejects. Example: MassHealth prefers brand Ventolin over generic albuterol.

glossary.short: Must fail a preferred drug first.
glossary.definition: The plan requires the patient to try (and fail, or be intolerant of) one or more preferred drugs before it will cover the one you want.

glossary.short: Tell the pharmacy not to substitute a generic.
glossary.definition: A note on the prescription instructing the pharmacist to dispense the exact brand you wrote, not a generic substitute. Needed when the plan's BOGL rule forces the brand.
Short-acting beta-agonist. The inhaler a patient carries and uses when they suddenly can't breathe well. Works in minutes; not a daily controller.

glossary.short: A daily steroid inhaler that calms inflammation.
glossary.definition: Inhaled corticosteroid. Taken every day to keep the airways from getting inflamed and twitchy. It prevents attacks; it does not relieve one in progress.

glossary.short: Inhalers have generics; biologics have biosimilars.
glossary.definition: Inhalers are small-molecule drugs, so cheaper copies are FDA-approved "generics" (e.g., Wixela Inhub for Advair). Injectable biologics like Xolair get "biosimilars" instead (e.g., Omlyclo) — a different FDA pathway.

preferredAgent.sig: 2 puffs every 4 to 6 hours as needed
preferredAgent.plainSig: Two puffs when you need it, no more than every 4 hours.

records.boglNote: MassHealth lists brand Ventolin HFA as Brand Preferred and PA-exempt, while the generic albuterol HFA inhaler requires prior authorization. Write Ventolin, dispense as written.
records.stepTherapy: Generic albuterol HFA requires a documented trial of an albuterol product available without PA (brand Ventolin HFA, or ProAir RespiClick).
records.verificationNote: Read directly off MHDL Table 23 and PA-37 (Rev. 01/26): Ventolin is Brand Preferred/PA-exempt; generic albuterol inhaler requires PA.

preferredAgent.sig: 2 inhalations twice daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Two breaths in, morning and night, every day.

records.stepTherapy: Alvesco, fluticasone propionate aerosol (age ≥12), and QVAR RediHaler require a documented trial of two inhaled corticosteroids first.
records.verificationNote: Step-therapy structure is verbatim from PA-37; the specific no-PA preferred ICS is inferred — confirm the preferred agent on the current MHDL.

preferredAgent.sig: 2 puffs twice daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Two puffs, morning and night, every day.

records.stepTherapy: AirDuo RespiClick requires a prior trial of fluticasone/salmeterol inhalation aerosol/powder (generic Advair). Symbicort, Advair, Breo, and Dulera are preferred without PA.
records.verificationNote: MHDL Table 23 marks Symbicort as Preferred Drug (PD), Brand Preferred, no PA; AirDuo step from PA-37 Section III.

preferredAgent.sig: 2 inhalations once daily
preferredAgent.plainSig: Two breaths in, once a day, same time each day.

records.stepTherapy: Tudorza requires a prior trial of Incruse, Spiriva Respimat, or tiotropium inhalation powder. Yupelri requires a prior trial of ipratropium nebulizer solution. LAMA is covered for documented COPD.
records.verificationNote: Step-therapy criteria verbatim from PA-37 Section X / II.3; LAMA is COPD-indicated per the FDA label.

preferredAgent.sig: 2 puffs every 4 to 6 hours as needed
preferredAgent.plainSig: Two puffs when you need it, no more than every 4 hours.

records.stepTherapy: Fluticasone propionate HFA requires members aged 12+ with asthma to fail BOTH preferred brands Arnuity Ellipta and QVAR RediHaler first (or document the need for an MDI + spacer). Covered without PA through age 11.

records.stepTherapy: Nebulized long-acting beta-agonists (Brovana/Perforomist) require a COPD diagnosis plus rationale for why a non-nebulized LABA cannot be used.
records.verificationNote: MGB 2026 6-Tier formulary places Spiriva HandiHaler at Tier 2; nebulized LABA PA from the Arformoterol/Formoterol policy (eff. 2025-09-01). LAMA is COPD-indicated.

guides.label: MD · Menopause HT
guides.tagline: Pick a plan and hormone type. Start with the green answer.

classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestogen combination
classes.description: Single-product estrogen + progestogen (e.g. Combipatch, Bijuva, Climara Pro). Out of scope pending a sourced read.
classes.plainDescription: One product that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

references.publisher: Maryland Department of Health, Office of Pharmacy Services

CareFirst — Drugs Requiring Prior Authorization

references.label: ACOG — Hormone Therapy for Menopausal Symptoms (patient FAQ)
references.publisher: American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists

references.label: The Menopause Society — 2022 Hormone Therapy Position Statement

glossary.definition: Insurer sign-off needed before the drug is covered.
Must fail a preferred drug first before the plan covers this one.
Estrogen, with a progestogen if you still have a uterus, to treat menopause symptoms.
Vaginal dryness and urinary symptoms of menopause, usually treated with low-dose local estrogen.
If you have a uterus, a progestogen is added to estrogen to keep the uterine lining safe.
Estrogen patches and gels are absorbed through the skin and carry a lower clot risk than estrogen pills.

preferredAgent.sig: Apply one 0.05 mg/24 hr patch twice weekly; rotate sites. Add a progestogen if the uterus is intact.
preferredAgent.sigShort: Estradiol 0.05 mg/24hr patch, apply twice weekly
preferredAgent.plainSig: Stick one patch on your lower belly and change it twice a week. Put each new patch on a slightly different spot.

records.verificationNote: MD Medicaid FFS PDL (eff. 1/1/2026, upd. 3/26/2026) does not manage this class as a PDL category, and the DAW6 brand-preferred list names no estrogen/progestogen — so the generic is covered as a standard benefit with no PDL-driven PA. The specific first-pass agent and dose are the clinically-standard choice (the plan names no preferred agent for an unmanaged class). Compounded products are excluded per MDH policy.

preferredAgent.sig: Take 200 mg by mouth at bedtime for 12–14 days each month (cyclic), or 100 mg nightly with continuous estrogen.
preferredAgent.sigShort: Micronized progesterone 100 mg PO QHS (cont.) / 200 mg x12–14 d/mo (cyclic)
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one capsule at bedtime. On a cycle, take it about two weeks a month; continuous, every night. It protects your uterine lining.

records.verificationNote: MD Medicaid FFS PDL (eff. 1/1/2026, upd. 3/26/2026) does not manage this class as a PDL category, and the DAW6 brand-preferred list names no estrogen/progestogen — so the generic is covered as a standard benefit with no PDL-driven PA. The specific first-pass agent and dose are the clinically-standard choice (the plan names no preferred agent for an unmanaged class). Compounded products are excluded per MDH policy.

preferredAgent.sig: Insert per the starting regimen, then 1 g intravaginally 1–3 times weekly for genitourinary syndrome of menopause.
preferredAgent.plainSig: Use the applicator to put a small amount of cream inside the vagina, about twice a week after the first weeks. It eases dryness and irritation.

alternatives.note: brand-only — may be higher tier on the dispensing MCO; confirm

records.verificationNote: Local vaginal estrogen is not a managed PDL category on MD Medicaid FFS; generic estradiol vaginal cream/tablet is a standard benefit with no PDL PA. Brand inserts/rings are not separately confirmed on the FFS PDL — confirm against the dispensing MCO. Compounded excluded.

preferredAgent.sig: Apply one 0.05 mg/24 hr patch to clean, dry skin of the lower abdomen weekly; rotate sites. Add a progestogen if the uterus is intact.
preferredAgent.plainSig: Stick one estrogen patch on your lower belly once a week, on a slightly different spot each time. If you still have your uterus, you also need a progesterone.

alternatives.note: Tier 2 — combo patch for an intact uterus

paRequired.drug: Estradiol patch/gel — members age 70+ (High Risk Medication)

records.verificationNote: Read off the CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 (pp. 119–120, upd. 6/1/2026): generic estradiol transdermal patch (weekly & twice-weekly), all strengths, is Tier 1; brand patches/gels/spray sit higher. A High Risk Medication PA applies to estrogens only for members age 70 and older (Beers-criteria review), not to typical menopausal patients.

preferredAgent.sig: For continuous estrogen: 100 mg by mouth at bedtime nightly. For cyclic: 200 mg at bedtime for 12 days each month. Take at bedtime (sedating).
preferredAgent.sigShort: Micronized progesterone 100 mg PO QHS (cont.) / 200 mg x12 d/mo (cyclic)
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one progesterone capsule at bedtime — it can make you drowsy. It protects your uterine lining while you're on estrogen.

alternatives.note: Tier 1 — combo for an intact uterus

records.verificationNote: Read off the CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 (Progestins section, ~p. 123): generic micronized progesterone 100/200 mg is Tier 1 with no PA and no step therapy.

preferredAgent.sig: Insert 2–4 g intravaginally daily for 1–2 weeks, then 1 g 1–3 times weekly for maintenance. Lowest effective dose.
preferredAgent.sigShort: Estradiol 0.01% vaginal cream, 1 g PV 2–3x/week (after induction)
preferredAgent.plainSig: Use the vaginal estrogen cream with the applicator — a short daily course at first, then about twice a week to keep the tissue healthy.

records.verificationNote: Read off the CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 (p. 121): estradiol vaginal cream 0.01% is Tier 1 with no flag (the age-70 HRM PA does not apply to the vaginal cream). Generic vaginal tablet Tier 1; Imvexxy Tier 2; Premarin vaginal Tier 3.

preferredAgent.sig: Apply one 0.05 mg/24 hr patch to clean, dry skin of the lower abdomen weekly; rotate sites; titrate to symptom control.
preferredAgent.plainSig: Stick one patch on your lower belly once a week. Move it to a slightly different spot each time.

records.verificationNote: Read off the KP Mid-Atlantic Marketplace Formulary (upd. 4/7/2026): the generic 'estradiol' line is Tiers 1–3 with no PA/ST/QL; the transdermal patch falls under this unrestricted generic line (corroborated by the HMO Mid/Large Employer formulary).

preferredAgent.sig: Take 2.5–5 mg by mouth daily with continuous estrogen, or 5–10 mg daily for 12–14 days each month (cyclic), for endometrial protection.
preferredAgent.sigShort: Medroxyprogesterone 2.5–5 mg PO daily (cont.) / 5–10 mg x12–14 d/mo (cyclic)
preferredAgent.plainSig: Take one tablet by mouth as your prescriber directs to protect your uterine lining while you're on estrogen.

paRequired.drug: Micronized progesterone (Prometrium generic) — clinically preferred, but PA on this plan

records.verificationNote: Read off the KP Mid-Atlantic Marketplace Formulary (4/7/2026): generic oral micronized progesterone is flagged PA + quantity limit on this plan (atypical), so generic medroxyprogesterone (Tiers 1/3, no restriction) is the first-pass that ships clean here. If micronized progesterone is clinically preferred (tolerability/lipid profile), expect a PA.

preferredAgent.sig: Insert 2–4 g intravaginally daily for 1–2 weeks, then taper to 1 g 1–3 times weekly for maintenance.
preferredAgent.plainSig: Use the applicator to place a small amount of estrogen cream inside the vagina — daily for a couple of weeks, then about twice a week.

records.verificationNote: Read off the KP Mid-Atlantic Marketplace Formulary (4/7/2026): the generic 'estradiol vaginal' line is Tiers 1–3 with no PA/ST. Femring is Tier 3, no PA.
